title: Perfect Fifth Shapes
description: Practice the perfect fifth shape across all string pairs, moving through common root notes. The root-fifth pattern is the single most common bass line in rock and pop.
instructions: The perfect fifth shape: root on one string, same fret + 2 frets on the next string up (skip no strings).
Start with E root (open E string). Its fifth is B at fret 2 on the A string.
Play: root on beat 1, fifth on beat 2, root on beat 3, fifth on beat 4.
Move to A root (open A string). Its fifth is E at fret 2 on the D string.
Move to D root (open D string). Its fifth is A at fret 2 on the G string.
Then try moveable shapes: G root (fret 3 on E string), fifth at fret 5 on A string.

tab.label: Root-fifth on E-A strings (E and A roots)
Moveable root-fifth — G, C, D (common rock progression)

variations.label: Musical: root-fifth-octave (adds the octave on the 'and of 2')

ensembleNotes: The root-fifth pattern works universally because the fifth is harmonically neutral — it doesn't clash with whether the chord is major or minor. You can play root-fifth over any chord without worrying about whether to play a major or minor third. It's the safest, most supportive thing a bassist can do, and it sounds great.
